answer: The Kubernetes control plane consists of kube-apiserver, etcd, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, and optionally cloud-controller-manager. These components make cluster-wide decisions and manage the overall state of the cluster.
etcd is Kubernetes' distributed key-value store. It stores cluster configuration, state information, and metadata required for the control plane to function reliably.

The kube-apiserver exposes the Kubernetes API and serves as the central communication hub for all cluster components. It processes API requests and validates, persists, and coordinates cluster state changes.
A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It represents one or more containers that share networking, storage resources, and a common execution context.
Yes. A Pod can contain multiple containers that run together and share networking and storage resources. This pattern is often used for sidecar and helper containers.
The kubelet is an agent that runs on each node. It ensures that containers described by Pod specifications are running and healthy on that node.
A Kubernetes Service provides a stable network endpoint for accessing a set of Pods. Services enable reliable communication even when Pods are created, deleted, or replaced.
Pods are ephemeral and can be recreated at any time. Services provide stable networking and service discovery so clients can consistently reach workloads regardless of Pod lifecycle changes.
A PersistentVolume is a cluster resource that provides durable storage independent of Pod lifecycles. It allows applications to retain data beyond the lifetime of individual containers.
A PersistentVolumeClaim is a request for storage made by a user or application. Kubernetes binds a claim to a suitable PersistentVolume that satisfies the requested storage requirements.
Node labels are key-value pairs attached to nodes. They are used to organize nodes and influence scheduling decisions by allowing workloads to target specific groups of nodes.
Cluster DNS provides name resolution for Services and other cluster resources. It enables workloads to discover and communicate with each other using stable DNS names instead of IP addresses.
Network plugins implement the Container Network Interface (CNI) specification. They allocate IP addresses to Pods and enable communication between Pods across the cluster.
Worker nodes typically run kubelet, a container runtime, and optionally kube-proxy. These components host and manage application workloads running as Pods.
Deployments provide declarative management for stateless applications. They support scaling, rolling updates, rollbacks, and self-healing through ReplicaSets.
The kube-scheduler watches for unscheduled Pods and selects appropriate nodes for them based on resource availability, scheduling policies, constraints, and affinity rules.
The kube-controller-manager runs controllers that continuously reconcile cluster state. Examples include the node controller, job controller, EndpointSlice controller, and ServiceAccount controller.
The cloud-controller-manager is used when Kubernetes runs on a cloud provider. It integrates Kubernetes with cloud-specific services such as load balancers, routes, and node management.
Pods are considered ephemeral because they can be created, terminated, rescheduled, or replaced at any time. Applications should avoid relying on a Pod's lifetime for data persistence.
Pod lifecycle phases include Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, and Unknown. These phases describe the current state of a Pod from scheduling through termination.

A Pod may remain in Pending state when Kubernetes cannot successfully schedule it to a node. Common causes include insufficient CPU or memory resources, node selector mismatches, untolerated taints, or unresolved PersistentVolumeClaims.
A Pod enters the Failed phase when one or more containers terminate unsuccessfully and are not expected to restart. This indicates that the Pod could not complete its intended work successfully.
Containers within the same Pod share the same network namespace and can communicate using localhost. They can also share storage volumes mounted into the Pod.

A Deployment performs rolling updates by gradually replacing old Pods with new ones. Kubernetes controls the update process using settings such as maxSurge and maxUnavailable to maintain application availability.
A Deployment can be rolled back to a previous revision. Kubernetes stores rollout history, allowing administrators to restore a previously working version when a deployment update causes issues.
If a Deployment update fails, Kubernetes continues monitoring rollout progress. Administrators can investigate the issue, pause the rollout, or roll back to a previous revision.

A Deployment maintains the desired state by continuously reconciling the actual number and configuration of Pods against the declared specification. ReplicaSets are used to ensure the desired number of replicas remains available.
A Deployment manages ReplicaSets, and ReplicaSets manage Pods. When a Deployment is updated, Kubernetes creates a new ReplicaSet and gradually shifts traffic from the old ReplicaSet to the new one.
Deployments should be used instead of directly creating Pods when managing stateless applications because they provide scaling, self-healing, rolling updates, and rollback capabilities.
ClusterIP exposes a Service internally within the cluster, while NodePort exposes the Service on a static port on each node. NodePort allows external access to the Service through node IP addresses.
A LoadBalancer Service provisions an external load balancer through the underlying cloud provider and exposes an application outside the cluster. It is commonly used for public-facing services.
kube-proxy maintains network rules on each node and routes traffic from Services to their associated Pod endpoints. It enables Service-based networking within the cluster.
An EndpointSlice is a Kubernetes resource that stores information about network endpoints backing a Service. It improves scalability and efficiency compared to the older Endpoints resource.
Services discover Pods through label selectors. Kubernetes continuously tracks matching Pods and updates EndpointSlices so traffic is routed to healthy endpoints.
Pods communicate across different nodes through the cluster networking model. Kubernetes networking ensures that every Pod can communicate with every other Pod without NAT.
The kube-scheduler determines Pod placement by evaluating resource availability, scheduling constraints, affinity rules, taints, tolerations, and other policies before selecting a node.
If no node has sufficient resources, the Pod remains in Pending state until resources become available or scheduling constraints are modified. Kubernetes will continue attempting to schedule the Pod.
Workloads can be restricted to specific nodes using node selectors, node affinity rules, or topology constraints. These mechanisms influence how the scheduler chooses target nodes.
Taints allow nodes to repel Pods, while tolerations allow Pods to be scheduled onto tainted nodes. Together they provide fine-grained control over workload placement.

PersistentVolumes are bound to PersistentVolumeClaims when a volume satisfies the claim's requested size, access modes, and storage requirements. Once bound, the claim gains exclusive access to that volume.

Kubernetes nodes communicate with the API server using authenticated and encrypted connections. Components such as kubelet use certificates and secure APIs to exchange information with the control plane.
Securing kubelet communication helps protect node-level operations and workload management. Unauthorized access to kubelet APIs could allow attackers to inspect, modify, or disrupt workloads.

The Konnectivity service provides secure network connectivity between control plane components and cluster nodes. It replaces older SSH tunnel mechanisms with a more scalable and maintainable architecture.

Kubernetes authenticates API requests using mechanisms such as certificates, bearer tokens, service accounts, and external identity providers. Authorization determines what authenticated users are allowed to do within the cluster using mechanisms like RBAC or ABAC.

Migrating to cgroup v2 requires a Linux kernel version 4.15 or later, a compatible container runtime (like containerd or CRI-O), and a kubelet configuration that supports it. Verification involves checking resource isolation, memory management changes, and testing workloads under the new runtime environment.

A Pod can remain Pending even when nodes appear healthy if scheduling constraints cannot be satisfied. Common causes include insufficient allocatable resources, node affinity requirements, taints without matching tolerations, or unbound PersistentVolumeClaims.

Services provide a stable virtual endpoint, EndpointSlices track the Pods backing a Service, and kube-proxy programs node-level networking rules. Together they enable traffic to be routed reliably from a Service to healthy Pods.

A Deployment rolling update involves multiple Kubernetes components. The Deployment controller creates and manages ReplicaSets, the scheduler places new Pods on nodes, kubelet starts containers, and networking components ensure traffic reaches healthy replicas during the rollout.

The control plane coordinates Pod lifecycle management through several components. The API server stores desired state, controllers reconcile resources, the scheduler assigns Pods to nodes, and kubelet ensures workloads run according to specification.

Migrating from cgroup v1 to cgroup v2 requires verifying kernel support, container runtime compatibility, kubelet compatibility, and node configuration. Administrators should validate workloads and cluster components before enabling cgroup v2 across the cluster.
